title: Is GPT-4 Good Enough to Evaluate Jokes?
research_questions: Can GPT-4 assess the funniness of jokes in a way that matches human ratings when evaluated by rankings rather than individual scores?
Which GPT-4 system descriptions or roles produce joke rankings closest to human funniness rankings?
How do different amounts and types of prompt instructions and examples affect GPT-4's joke evaluations?

datasets_used.name: ExPUNations (ExPun)
datasets_used.size: 1500 jokes; 200 jokes and respective average scores used to generate the suggested system description

methods_proposed.name: Suggested description (SG) via many-shot prompting
methods_proposed.summary: The paper proposes generating a reusable GPT-4 system description by prompting GPT-4 once with 200 jokes and their average scores, then using the resulting description for later joke evaluation without including the examples in every inference.

methods_proposed.name: Ranking-based GPT-4 humor evaluation methodology
methods_proposed.summary: Instead of comparing raw 1-to-5 funniness scores directly, the paper compares human and GPT-4 joke rankings using Spearman rank correlation to reduce scaling effects and focus on evaluator behavior.

humor_theories.detail: The four humor styles from Martin et al. (2003)—affiliative, self-enhancing, aggressive, and self-defeating—are used as GPT-4 system descriptions.

evaluation_methods.detail: GPT-4 rates jokes on a 1-to-5 funniness scale and returns rankings from least to most funny under 7 system descriptions and 5 instruction levels.

Average human funniness scores from the Sun et al. (2022) annotations are used as the reference ranking; the cited annotators had to correctly label 80% of 20 manually annotated samples to qualify as reliable evaluators.
Spearman rank correlation coefficient is used to compare the joke rankings derived from human scores and GPT-4 scores.

headline_results.finding: The suggested system description generated from many-shot prompting produced the most positive correlation with human joke rankings among the tested system descriptions.
The evaluation covered all combinations of 7 system descriptions and 5 levels of prompt instructions.
headline_results.value: 35 combinations; 10 different samples of 5 jokes for each; totalling 1750 jokes

headline_results.finding: The extra-example instruction condition included examples in a score range that appeared in a notable portion of the dataset.
headline_results.metric: share of jokes rated in range [2.2-2.4]

headline_results.finding: Most jokes were low-scoring relative to the examples for scores 3 and 5, limiting the usefulness of those instructions.
headline_results.metric: number of jokes scored more than 3 by human evaluators

key_findings: GPT-4 with the many-shot-generated suggested description (SG) ranked jokes closest to humans, but the reported correlation was still weak, between 0.16 and 0.31.
Aggressive humour (AG) and affiliative humour (AH) system descriptions showed a very weak positive correlation with human rankings.

Self-enhancing humour (SE), self-defeating humour (SD), the naive humour expert description (HE), and no description (NONE) presented no correlation with human counterparts because their intervals intersected zero.
The baseline instruction prompt without detailed examples or explanations already showed positive correlation with human rankings.

Only the prompt with explanations, examples, and extra examples (EXP_EXA_EXT) increased the average Spearman coefficients above the baseline; prompts with only examples, only explanations, or both reduced the average coefficients.
The authors argue that ranking comparisons are more robust than direct score comparisons for non-deterministic models such as GPT-4 because they reduce scaling problems.
limitations: The best reported correlations are weak, between 0.16 and 0.31.
The experiments use 1500 jokes extracted from the Sun et al. (2022) dataset, with samples of 5 jokes for each prompt condition.
GPT-4 is restricted to 8192 tokens, which limits the number of jokes that can be included as examples when generating system descriptions.
Setting temperature to 0 in GPT-4 does not guarantee deterministic behavior, although the authors state it makes responses more robust with less variability.
The instruction examples for scores 1, 3, and 5 were poorly matched to the dataset distribution because only one joke scored more than 3 by human evaluators.
safety_ethics_notes: The paper notes that the source annotations include offensiveness and that the suggested system description is not particularly fond of offensive or inappropriate content; it does not include a dedicated safety or ethics discussion.

human_samples.population: Human evaluators from the Sun et al. (2022) ExPUNations annotation dataset reused by this paper.
human_samples.recruitment: The paper states that Sun et al. (2022) recruited human evaluators; no platform or recruitment details are reported here. Evaluators had to correctly label 80% of 20 manually annotated samples to qualify as reliable evaluators.
human_samples.observations: The current paper extracted 1500 jokes and used average human funniness scores from the prior annotations; Sun et al. annotations also included understandability, offensiveness, and intended joke labels. The number of ratings/annotations is not reported in this paper.
human_samples.detail: No new human participants were recruited for this study; human data are inherited from the cited dataset.

judges.name: Sun et al. (2022) human evaluators
judges.detail: Their average funniness scores are used to derive the human joke rankings against which GPT-4 rankings/scores are compared.

judges.name: GPT-4
judges.detail: GPT-4 rates jokes on a 1-to-5 funniness scale and returns rankings under 7 system descriptions and 5 instruction levels. GPT-4 also generated the suggested system description once from 200 jokes and their average scores.

judges.name: Spearman rank correlation coefficient
judges.detail: Used to compare rankings derived from human average funniness scores and GPT-4 scores/rankings.

generation_selection.detail: A GPT-4 prompt containing 200 randomly sampled jokes and their respective average scores was executed just once; the unique generated system description was used for all experiments. No alternative generated descriptions or selection procedure are reported.

generation_selection.n: 1 per prompted 5-joke sample, as described; no best-of-N reported
generation_selection.detail: For each combination of system description and instruction level, GPT-4 produced ratings/rankings for sampled sets of 5 jokes. The paper does not describe generating multiple candidate evaluations and selecting among them.

model_provenance.name: OpenAI GPT-4
model_provenance.detail: The paper refers to GPT-4 and GPT-4 chat-mode system descriptions, but does not report an API snapshot/version, endpoint, access mode, or inference dates.

inference_budgets.scope: GPT-4 joke evaluation/ranking across 7 system descriptions × 5 instruction levels
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: One GPT-4 rating/ranking output per prompted sample is described; no multiple candidates or best-of-N selection are reported.
inference_budgets.token_budget: The paper notes GPT-4 is restricted to 8192 tokens, but does not report per-call input/output token counts or max_tokens settings.
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: temperature=0; top P=1; frequency penalty=0; presence penalty=0
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: No numeric compute or cost reported.
inference_budgets.detail: The study randomly selected 10 different samples of 5 jokes for each of 35 combinations, totaling 1750 joke presentations; the same joke could be sampled more than once. Prompts asked GPT-4 to rate jokes from 1 to 5 and rank/sort them from least to most funny.

inference_budgets.scope: GPT-4 generation of the suggested system description (SG)
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: 1 generated system description; no alternatives reported.
inference_budgets.token_budget: Prompt used 200 randomly sampled jokes and their respective average scores; authors note GPT-4's 8192-token restriction as limiting the number of examples.
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Not separately reported for the SG-generation call; the paper reports temperature=0, top P=1, frequency penalty=0, and presence penalty=0 for OpenAI GPT-4 experiments.
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: Authors state the reusable SG description is cheaper than many-shot prompting because examples need not be included for every inference, but provide no numeric cost.
inference_budgets.detail: The 200 jokes and scores used in this prompt are omitted from the paper.

human_baselines.description: Average human funniness scores from Sun et al. (2022) are used to create human rankings for the same joke texts sampled from the 1500-joke dataset; GPT-4 rankings/scores are compared to these rankings with Spearman correlation.
human_baselines.detail: The comparison is on the same joke items and funniness construct, but the human ratings are from a prior dataset rather than newly collected under identical 5-joke prompt conditions.

contamination_risk.basis: The evaluated jokes and human scores come from public prior datasets cited as Sun et al. (2022) and SemEval/Hossain et al., predating the 2023 GPT-4 study/release context. The paper reports no decontamination test or private held-out benchmark. In addition, the proposed SG system description is generated from 200 randomly sampled jokes and average scores from the same 1500-joke dataset, while no hold-out exclusion or overlap check with evaluation samples is reported. Evaluation samples are also repeatedly drawn from the same pool, with the paper noting that the same joke can be sampled more than once.

llm_judge_dependence.affected_claims: All headline claims about whether GPT-4 can evaluate jokes similarly to humans, which system descriptions perform best, and which instruction levels improve correlations.
llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: Average human funniness scores from the prior Sun et al. (2022) dataset provide the reference ranking; no new human validation of GPT-4 judgments is reported.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: GPT-4 judgments are one side of every reported human-vs-model rank-correlation result, and GPT-4 also creates the many-shot-derived SG system description that is central to the best-performing condition.

reporting_gaps: The paper does not report the number of human evaluators, number of ratings per joke, demographics, geography, recruitment platform, compensation, or language background of annotators.
The paper summarizes the prior human annotation qualification test but does not report blinding, full annotation protocol, or aggregation details beyond using average funniness scores.
Dataset language(s) are not explicitly stated in this paper.
GPT-4 version/snapshot, API endpoint, access mode, and inference dates are not reported.
Exact 200 jokes and scores used to generate the SG system description are omitted.
No random seed is reported for selecting the 200 SG examples or the 10 evaluation samples per condition.
No hold-out split is reported between SG-generation examples and evaluation samples; possible overlap is not ruled out.
The exact sampled joke IDs and whether the same sampled sets were reused across system descriptions/instruction levels are not reported.
The exact number of GPT-4 API calls, retries, failures, malformed outputs, or parsing/cleanup procedures is not reported.
Per-call max output tokens, actual token usage, runtime, and numeric cost are not reported.
No GPT-4 training-data contamination or benchmark-exposure analysis is reported.
No code, data, or artifact URL is provided in the paper.
